output.final_deliverable:
I attempted to follow your mandate, but the required web search and visit results are not present in the merged-results data I was given. Because my response must be based only on evidence extracted from search and visit results, I cannot invent or guess URLs, paper details, government documents, or organization statements. What is missing: (A) the initial news-article search results and the visited news article page, and (B) any subsequent search/visit results for (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization’s position statement. I can proceed in one of two ways (pick one):

Option A — I will run live web searches now, open the best news article about climate change, then follow links and visit pages to extract and cite concrete details for each required item (the original news article, one research paper, one government policy document, one organization position). I will document the exact search queries and the visit chain and include the visited URLs as inline citations.

Option B — You provide a specific news article URL to start from (or paste the article text). I will follow links from that article and extract and cite the three required items, documenting the exploration path.

Tell me which option you prefer (I recommend Option A). If you pick Option A, I will run the searches now and return the full, evidence-backed exploration with URLs and summaries.
output.action_summary: No search/visit evidence was available in the inputs, so I cannot produce the requested evidence-backed chain yet. I outlined what is missing and offered two ways to proceed: I can run live searches now and produce the required documented chain (recommended), or you can supply a starting news-article URL for me to follow. Please choose one.

534fdfc6-d791-4ef9-819f-88e14859631f.title:
Start at a news article about climate change (find one via search). From that article, follow links to find: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each, provide the URL, a summary of the content, and explain how it relates to the original article. Document your exploration path.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

details.mandate:
Start at a news article about climate change (find one via search). From that article, follow links to find: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each, provide the URL, a summary of the content, and explain how it relates to the original article. Document your exploration path.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

e3b68bdb-8074-448d-aa0d-a06ed1625a20.title: Sequential plan: find a news article about climate change, visit it, then follow its links to identify (1) a scientific paper, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement; extract URLs, summaries, relations, and document exploration path.

internal_plan.goal: Start from a news article about climate change (found via search). From that article, follow links to locate and extract details for: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each resource provide: URL, concise summary, explanation of how it relates to the news article, and an explicit exploration path (which pages were visited and why).
target_facts_to_extract: A recent, credible news article URL and title about climate change
From that article: outgoing links (URLs) and context around each link
One linked scientific research paper/study (or a research paper cited directly by the article) — URL, title, authors, publication/source, date, main findings
One linked government policy document — URL, issuing agency, date, scope, key policy points
One linked organizational position statement — URL, organization name, date, key positions and recommendations

For each item above: a short summary (2–6 sentences) and a clear statement connecting it back to the original news article (how the article used/cited the source, supported/contradicted it, or relied on its findings).
A documented exploration path listing each visited URL and why it was visited (search → article → links → each resource).
likely_sources_to_search: News outlets: BBC, The Guardian, New York Times, Washington Post, Reuters, AP, The Conversation (science-based news)
Scientific journals/repositories: Nature, Science, PNAS, Environmental Research Letters, arXiv, PubMed, Google Scholar
Government sites: EPA (epa.gov), UK GOV (gov.uk), White House (whitehouse.gov), UNFCCC (unfccc.int), IPCC (ipcc.ch)
Organizations: WWF, Greenpeace, NRDC, Climate Action Network, American Meteorological Society, IPCC author org pages
verification_steps: Use web search (search tool) to find a recent climate change news article (query includes date range and reputable news source terms).
Visit the chosen news article (visit tool) to extract article title, publication date, author, key claims, and outbound links (and surrounding sentences where link appears).

From links extracted, prioritize direct links to scientific publications, government domains, and organizational pages. If the article cites a study without linking directly, follow citation text (journal name, DOI) using search and then visit the paper.
Visit each selected resource (visit tool) and extract concrete metadata and key findings/policy points/position statements.
Record the exact URLs visited and the text snippets used as evidence.
Compile findings: for each resource provide URL, 3–6 sentence summary, explicit relation to the news article, and exploration path (sequence of visited URLs with reason).
measurable_output_fields: original_article: {url, title, publication, date, author, 2–4 sentence summary}
scientific_paper: {url, title, authors, venue, date, 3–6 sentence summary, how_it_relates_to_article}
government_policy: {url, issuing_body, title, date, 3–6 sentence summary, how_it_relates_to_article}
organization_statement: {url, organization, title, date, 3–6 sentence summary, how_it_relates_to_article}
exploration_path: ordered list of visited URLs with brief reason for each visit and notes on what was extracted

details.why_this_step_reduces_uncertainty: Following this sequential plan ensures we (a) pick a concrete news article found via search (so we have a reproducible starting point), (b) read the full article to find the exact links or citations the article relies on (visiting is required because search snippets don't show link context), and (c) visit each linked resource to extract authoritative, citable content and directly verify how the news piece used those sources. This approach minimizes guesswork about which sources the article referenced and provides verifiable evidence (visited URLs and extracted text/snippets).

details.intent: Find a recent reputable news article about climate change (news report or analysis) that contains outbound links to sources.
query_examples: climate change article links to study 'climate' 'study' 2024 site:bbc.com
recent climate change news article links to research 'study' 'journal' 2024
details.measurable_result: A shortlist of candidate news article URLs (3–6), with titles and snippets, from which one will be chosen for visits.

details.intent: Visit the chosen news article URL to read full content and extract outbound links and surrounding sentences that reference sources.
details.measurable_result: Original article metadata (url, title, date, author) and a list of outbound links (URLs) with the sentence or paragraph context for each link.

details.intent: From the article's outbound links and citations, identify candidate links for: (a) scientific paper/study, (b) government policy document, (c) organization position statement. If article cites a paper but doesn't link, search for the cited paper by title/DOI.
details.measurable_result: Three selected URLs (one per category), with rationale for selection.

details.intent: Visit each of the three selected URLs (scientific paper, government policy doc, organization statement) to extract required metadata and short summaries and note how each was used in the article.
details.measurable_result: For each resource: url, title, author/issuing body, date, 3–6 sentence summary, and explicit connection to the original article (quote or paraphrase of how the article referenced it).

details.intent: Assemble the final deliverable: original article details, each resource with summary and relation, and the documented exploration path listing each visited URL and what was extracted.
details.measurable_result: Final structured output ready for user: URLs, summaries, relations, and exploration path.

constraints_and_notes: Must run search then visit (per benchmark rule). Do not rely solely on search snippets for content extraction.
Prefer a recent (within last 1–2 years) news article for topical relevance, but any credible article is acceptable if it contains outbound links to credible sources.

If the news article links to a paywalled paper, try to find an open-access preprint or the journal abstract and use that for extraction; if necessary, use the article's citation (journal/title/DOI) to find a public source.
details.parent_goal:
Start at a news article about climate change (find one via search). From that article, follow links to find: (1) a scientific research paper or study, (2) a government policy document, and (3) an organization's position statement. For each, provide the URL, a summary of the content, and explain how it relates to the original article. Document your exploration path.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

reasons: The agent did not produce the required outputs: no news article URL, no scientific paper, no government policy document, and no organization position statement were provided.

The agent correctly refused to fabricate information when search/visit evidence was unavailable, which is appropriate and responsible.
The agent offered two clear next steps (run live searches or accept a user-provided starting URL), enabling proper completion if the user chooses one.

details.exploration_quality: Insufficient. The response responsibly flagged missing evidence and proposed valid options to continue, but it did not complete the exploration or supply the required documents, summaries, relationships to the original article, or a documented path. To meet the task, the agent must execute one of the proposed options and return concrete, cited results.
